title: Personal Pronouns & Possessive Adjectives
description: Learn Czech personal pronouns (já, ty, on, ona, my, vy, oni) and possessive adjectives (můj, tvůj, jeho, její, náš, váš)

exercises.prompt: Match the English pronoun to its Czech equivalent: 'we'

data.explanation: my = we. The Czech personal pronouns are: já (I), ty (you sg.), on (he), ona (she), ono (it), my (we), vy (you pl.), oni/ony (they).

exercises.prompt: Choose the correct possessive adjective: '___ pes' (my dog, masculine)

data.question_cz: Vyberte správné přivlastňovací zájmeno: '___ pes' (můj pes, mužský rod)
data.question_en: Choose the correct possessive adjective: '___ pes' (my dog, masculine)
data.explanation: můj is used before masculine singular nouns (můj pes = my dog, můj dům = my house). For feminine and neuter singular, use moje (moje kniha, moje auto).

data.grammar_note: její (her) is invariable — it does not change for gender or number. It is the same for masculine, feminine, and neuter nouns.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank with the correct possessive adjective: 'To je ___ auto.' (our car, neuter)

data.explanation: For neuter singular nouns like auto, use naše (our). náš is for masculine singular (náš pes), while naše is for feminine and neuter singular (naše kniha, naše auto).

exercises.prompt: Which possessive adjective means 'your' (singular, for a masculine noun)?

data.question_cz: Které přivlastňovací zájmeno znamená 'tvůj' (jednotné číslo, mužský rod)?
data.question_en: Which possessive adjective means 'your' (singular, for a masculine noun)?
data.explanation: tvůj = your (singular, masculine). tvoje = your (singular, feminine/neuter). váš = your (plural, masculine). vaše = your (plural, feminine/neuter).

exercises.prompt: Translate to English: 'On je z Prahy.'

data.grammar_note: on = he. Czech subject pronouns can be omitted when the verb ending makes the subject clear: 'Je z Prahy.' also means 'He is from Prague.'

exercises.prompt: Arrange the words to form: 'This is my book.'

data.explanation: To je moje kniha. = This is my book. kniha is feminine, so we use moje (not můj, which is for masculine nouns).

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank with the correct subject pronoun: '___ jsme studenti.' (We are students.)

data.explanation: my = we. However, in everyday Czech the pronoun is usually dropped: 'Jsme studenti.' is more natural and equally correct.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence is more natural in everyday Czech?

data.question_cz: Která věta je přirozenější v běžné češtině?
data.question_en: Which sentence is more natural in everyday Czech?
data.explanation: In Czech, subject pronouns are usually dropped because the verb conjugation already shows the person. 'Jsem student.' (I am a student) is the natural form. 'Já jsem student.' is grammatically correct but used only for emphasis.

exercises.prompt: Listen and write what you hear.

data.note: auto is a neuter noun, so we use moje (my). Compare: můj pes (masc), moje kniha (fem), moje auto (neut).

exercises.prompt: Practice pronouncing the Czech personal pronouns.

data.target_text: já, ty, on, ona, ono, my, vy, oni
data.translation_en: I, you, he, she, it, we, you (pl.), they

exercises.answer_key: já, ty, on, ona, ono, my, vy, oni

exercises.prompt: Review: What are the Czech numbers 1-4?

data.question_cz: Která řada je správná pro čísla 1-4?
data.question_en: Which sequence is correct for the numbers 1-4?
data.explanation: jeden (1), dva (2), tři (3), čtyři (4). These are the cardinal numbers 1-4. Note: dva has a feminine form dvě (dvě knihy = two books), but dva is the default masculine form.
